它是互联网上最简单的网站之一：白色背景上的黑色文字，一些占用带宽的图形和少量小照片（如果有的话）。但是这种极简主义并没有阻止它在去年赢得大约1200亿次页面浏览量。
 该站点是Wikipedia，这是今天庆祝其20周年的免费百科全书。它的用户不会聚集到Wikipedia观看病毒视频，发布孩子的照片或在评论中争论。取而代之的是，他们来寻找关于300种语言（甚至世界语）的惊人话题的干爽无味的信息。它远非闪烁，但Wikipedia在分裂后的真相中具有不可思议的价值。错误信息泛滥的世界，唐纳德·特朗普总统连任的谎言导致美国国会大厦发生暴力骚乱，炸死5人。
   在捐赠的支持下，由全球编辑志愿者社区进行管理，Wikipedia代表了互联网的一个平静，理性的角落，并且是在庞大项目上开展全球合作的典范。即使偶尔遇到事实失误，我们也比以往任何时候都更需要它。
  您可以称我为Wikipedia超级粉丝。即使我几天前才意识到我们拥有相同的生日，但我还是骄傲地戴着这本书的徽章。我不记得是什么时候第一次发现该网站的，尽管我认为它位于非常适合基本设计的拨号上网连接上。但是自从阅读了第一篇文章以来，我每天至少潜水一次，该应用已成为手机上使用最多的应用之一。而且我还有很多工作要做-这个英文网站拥有超过620万篇文章（相当于2,821册印刷量），每天发布597余篇新文章。用其他语言加上，该网站有5500万篇文章。
  我之所以喜欢它，是因为Wikipedia是互联网上的救赎之举之一，使在线世界摆脱了它经常滑倒的地狱般的漏洞。除了讨厌仇恨言论和点击诱饵标题外，还可以立即访问填充我的知识空白的干净信息。如果我正在读书，并且遇到陌生的概念，地点，人物或事物，我通常会放下这本书，然后跳到Wikipedia以在继续阅读之前了解更多信息（在Amazon Kindle上阅读时除外） ，为您完成工作）。
 也许这么快就能获得所有这些信息让我很不耐烦-奇怪的是我曾经去图书馆看东西-但也很满意。我什至有时甚至只是在网站上浏览我最喜欢的主题之一。它是Twitter末日滚动令人沮丧的经历的完美在线解毒剂，我提出了一些我以后可以参考的内容。您是否想知道架空索道和缆车的区别（为什么不呢？）？好吧，维基百科可以告诉你。在中立的角度来看都是存在的网站要求的写作风格（是的，甚至还有一篇文章）。
  维基媒体非营利组织维基媒体基金会（Wikimedia Foundation）的首席产品官托比·内格林（Toby Negrin）说，人们对维基百科的贡献越多，他们变得越中立。 ＆＃34;免费知识是我们工作的核心。自愿的Wikipedian建立了政策和流程，以确保编辑植根于可验证的事实中，并且确保阅读Wikipedia文章的举动引起对所阅读信息的批判性思考。＆＃34; 
除亚马逊以外，其他科技公司也依赖维基百科的全面性。 YouTube会从满载阴谋论的视频中获取信息，Siri用它来回答您的问题，Google将网站的文章放在许多搜索结果顶部的框中，而Facebook依靠它在出版物发表时提供背景信息您会在墙上贴上一个新故事（在圈子中寻找小巧的i＆＃34;）。所以，真的，不仅仅是我。有时人们会尝试在系统上玩游戏并重写事实，但是任何违反网站详细标准的事情都会使Wikipedia编辑非常快。
  Wikipedia的声誉不仅来自其信息，还来自全世界撰写和编辑文章的编辑人员（所有需要注册帐户的志愿者）以及负责监视所有内容的管理员。人数众多-每个月有280,000多位编辑-他们一直很忙。编辑速度为每秒1.9次编辑，而该英语站点上个月获得了600万次编辑。
 他们也很快。当我在本周三撰写众议院第二次投票选举弹imp特朗普的程序时，随着事件的进展，相关的维基百科页面正在发生变化。到下午5点PT，在投票大约四个小时后，它有5600多个字长，被146次引用并在不断增长。但是速度不仅仅适用于当前事件。我的一个最喜欢的例子是航空公司宣布一条新航线（我告诉过我我是一个书呆子）。我保证，如果您在几分钟后浏览航空公司的文章，将会有更新。
 当然，任何人都可以编辑的网站没有问题。例如，其编辑人员之间存在巨大的性别差距。错误，编辑战争，网站上的故意破坏以及其他争议也得到了很好的记录，即使是Wikipedia本身通常也很详尽的形式。尽管如此，大多数Wikipedia的页面还是充分引用了第三方资源以及指向其他Wikipedia文章的链接（很容易消失在兔子洞中），研究人员对此网站表示赞赏。可靠性。当确实发生故意破坏行为时，Wikimedia会在五分钟之内将其解决，您可以查看页面的编辑历史记录。
 该网站针对热键主题设置了更严格的编辑器标准，例如禁止从全新帐户中进行更改，并且为2020年选举创建了虚假信息工作队。网页会定期标记呼叫，以进行更好的编辑，更多文档并删除有偏见的语言，并且信息太少，入站链接或访问量很少的网页可以删除。回顾我为CNET编写的电话时，专门针对我的简短Wikipedia文章就是这种情况（我发誓我没有写）。一天在那里，然后*捣碎*它消失了。
 今天，我不应该责怪那个编辑偏爱我最喜欢的网站之一的编辑角。我知道他们只是在做改善网站的工作。即使是粉丝，我也同意Wikipedia不应被用作学生，学者或新闻工作者的主要资源。但这对于作为经过更严格审查的原始书籍，论文或报告的跳板是非常有价值的。换句话说，在这里找到您真正的开胃菜，然后去某个地方学习主菜。
 维基百科可能很无聊，但这是我们拥有的一些最聪明的互联网。周年快乐，维基百科。还有更多。
note_en:
It's one of the simplest sites on the internet: black text on a white background, a few bandwidth-hogging graphics and a handful of small photos, if any at all. But that minimalism didn't stop it from winning  around 120 billion page views last year.
 The site is  Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia that celebrates  its 20th anniversary today. Its users don't flock to Wikipedia to watch viral videos, post photos of their kids or argue in the comments. Instead they come seeking dry and dispassionately-presented information on a staggering array of topics in 300 languages (even  Esperanto). It's far from scintillating, but Wikipedia is incredibly valuable in a divisive "post-truth" world where   misinformation is rampant and where  a lie that President  Donald Trump won   reelection led to   a violent riot at the US Capitol that killed five people.
   Supported  by donations and managed by a worldwide community of editor volunteers, Wikipedia represents  a calm, rational corner of the internet and a model for how global collaboration on a sprawling project can work. And even with its occasional factual stumbles, we need it now more than ever.
  You can call me a Wikipedia superfan. It's a nerdy badge I wear proudly, even if I realized only a few days ago that we share the same birthday. I don't remember when I first discovered the site, though I imagine it was on a grinding   dial-up internet connection well-suited to the basic design. But since reading my first article, I dive in at least once a day and  the app has become one of the most-used on my phone. And I have a lot to get through -- the English site has more than  6.2 million articles (which amount to  2,821 printed volumes), with more than 597 new articles posting each day. Add in the other languages and the site reaches 55 million articles.
  I'm a fan because Wikipedia is one of the internet's saving graces, lifting the online world out of the hellish hole that it often slithers down. Instead of  hate speech and click bait headlines, there's just instant access to clean information that fills my knowledge gaps. If I'm reading a book and come across an unfamiliar concept, place, person or thing, I'll usually put the book down and jump to Wikipedia to learn more before reading on (except when reading on an  Amazon Kindle, which  does the work for you).
 Maybe having all of that information available so quickly has made me impatient -- it's weird to think that I used to go to a library to look up something -- but it's also satisfying. I even sometimes go on the site just to browse through one of my favorite topics. It's a perfect online antidote to the dispiriting experience of Twitter doomscrolling and I come away with something I can reference later. Do you want to know the difference between an  aerial tramway and a  gondola lift (and why  wouldn't you)? Well, Wikipedia can tell you. It's there for the taking, all in the "neutral point of view" writing style that the site requires (yes, there's  even an article for that).
  Toby Negrin, the chief product officer at the Wikimedia Foundation, the nonprofit that hosts Wikipedia, says the more people contribute to Wikipedia, the more neutral they become. "Free knowledge is at the core of what we do. Volunteer Wikipedians have built policies and processes to ensure that edits are rooted in verifiable facts, and that the act of reading a Wikipedia article invites critical thinking about the information you read."
 Beyond Amazon, other tech companies rely on Wikipedia's thoroughness.   YouTube sources it on conspiracy theory-laden videos, Siri uses it to answer your questions, Google puts the site's articles in the box at the top of many search results and Facebook relies on it to give the background on a publication when you post a new story to your wall (look for the small "i" in the circle). So, really, it's not just me. Sometimes people try to game the system and rewrite facts, but anything that violates the site's  detailed standards tends to rile up the Wikipedia editors pretty quickly.
  Wikipedia's reputation comes not just from its information, but also the worldwide community of editors (all volunteers who need to register for an account) who write and edit articles and  administrators who watch over it all. It's a lot of people -- more than 280,000 editors a month -- and they keep busy. Edits are made at  a rate of 1.9 edits per second with the English site getting  6 million edits last month.
 They're also fast. As I was writing this Wednesday while the House of Representatives   was in the process of voting to impeach Trump for the second time,  the related Wikipedia page was changing as events unfolded. By 5 p.m. PT, about four hours after the vote, it was more than 5,600 words long with 146 citations and growing. But the speed isn't just for current events. A favorite example of mine is when  an airline announces a new route (I told you   I was a nerd). I guarantee that if you go to the airline's article minutes later, the update will be there.
 Of course, a site that anyone can edit isn't without problems. There's  a massive gender gap among its editors, for one. Errors, editing wars,  vandalism on the site and other   controversies also are well-documented,  even by Wikipedia itself in its typically thorough form. Still, the majority of Wikipedia's pages are well-cited with third-party sources and links to other Wikipedia articles (it's easy to disappear down a rabbit hole) and researchers  have commended the site's reliability. When vandalism does occur, Wikimedia says it's addressed within five minutes and you  can view a page's edit history.
 The site has set stricter editor standards for hot-button topics, like banning changes from brand new accounts, and   it created a disinformation task force for the 2020 election. Pages are regularly flagged with calls for better editing, more documentation and the removal of biased language and pages with too little information, inbound links or traffic can be deleted. That was the case for the brief Wikipedia article dedicated to me (I swear I didn't write it) back when   I was reviewing phones for CNET. One day it was there, and then *poof* it was gone.
 Today, I don't blame the editor who nuked my miniscule corner of one of my favorite sites. I know they were just doing their job of making the site better. Even as a fan, I'd agree Wikipedia shouldn't be used as a primary resource for students, academics, or journalists. But it is valuable as a springboard to an original book, paper or report that has faced greater scrutiny. In other words, get your factual starter appetizer here, then go somewhere for your main course.
 Wikipedia may be boring, but it's some of the sanest internet we have. Happy anniversary, Wikipedia. Here's to many more.
title: 在后事实世界中，我们比以往任何时候都更需要维基百科
title_en: In a post-truth world, we need Wikipedia more than ever

note:
随着美国的“选举日”变成“选举周”，Facebook，Twitter和YouTube发现自己在努力避免灾难。唐纳德·特朗普总统宣布获胜，毫无根据地指称选举欺诈，尽管战场上的各州仍在继续邮寄选票。总统的支持者在社交媒体上反驳了他的虚假主张。
 但是，在Wikipedia上，2020年美国总统大选页面就像一座堡垒一样被锁定。直到下个星期六新闻媒体宣布获胜，比赛才在页面上被取消。成功的部分原因是维基百科背后的非营利组织Wikimedia Foundation成立了虚假信息工作队。
   尽管所有主要的技术平台都为选举进行了热烈的准备，但美国投票期间Wikipedia缺乏丑闻，这突显了该网站的立场：功能完善的开放互联网。相比之下，Big Tech平台尽管拥有丰富的工程资源和雄厚的资金，却始终充斥着错误的信息。
 ＆＃34;维基百科是人类历史上最伟大的集体行为之一，＆＃34;维基媒体基金会幕僚长瑞安·默克利（Ryan Merkley）在接受采访时说。他指的是由成千上万的志愿者组成的网站军队，他们编辑页面并保持最新状态。这是透明且负责的。
 星期五是Wikipedia成立20周年。该网站的生日提醒着人们，在互联网和现实世界特别混乱的时期内，它的耐用性。亲特朗普极端分子在美国国会大厦发动了一场暴动，这在很大程度上是在线计划的。总统和极右翼人物用推特，Facebook和YouTube播种了多年的不信任种子，激怒了暴徒。
 自袭击发生以来的几天里，社交平台已被人们深深地迷住了。 Facebook，YouTube和Twitter都以某种方式控制了特朗普，而Twitter则永久禁止他。 Facebook取消了使用集会号召停止发帖的帖子。 YouTube已禁止前特朗普顾问史蒂夫·班农（Steve Bannon）的播客，而推特（Twitter）已清除了超过7万名QAnon奉献者的账户，他们毫无根据地认为政府是由一群崇拜撒旦的性贩子经营的。
  维基百科并不完美。它的页面容易被人为破坏，该网站通常是页面编辑者的恶作剧和内f的目标。但是Wikipedia上的失败并没有给民主带来危险。在线百科全书社区比主流和社交媒体更好地提供了事实核查。哥伦比亚新​​闻评论周四写道。 
维基百科是由商人吉米·威尔士（Jimmy Wales）于20年前创立的，如今在全球范围内发展迅猛。该网络拥有超过5500万页的300多种语言。默克利说，去年它的访问量达到了创纪录的水平，每月访问量为150亿次。根据网络排名公司Alexa的说法，它是互联网上第13大最受欢迎的网站。
 在当今信息日趋商品化的世界中，维基百科的模型始终基于这样的信念，即知识属于所有人。威尔士在一份声明中说。 ＆＃34; Wikipedia迫使我们走出回音室，并争辩对世界的共同理解是什么样子。
 维基百科曾经被认为是一个笑话，因为网络上的任何人都可以编辑其页面，这些年来，它建立了惊人的权威。 Google在其搜索结果中直接使用了Wikipedia的摘要。由Google拥有的YouTube链接到其信息面板中的Wikipedia页面，这些页面是简短的内容，出现在虚假或误导性视频下，是YouTube努力揭露错误信息的一部分。当人们向他们提问时，Alexa和Siri等语音助手有时会朗诵Wikipedia段落。
 一次又一次地证明Wikipedia做得非常好。他们的策展和控制良好，加州大学伯克利分校信息学院教授Coye Cheshire说，他从早期开始就对Wikipedia进行研究。今天，它变得更加值得信赖，因为他们已经证明自己值得信赖。
 默克利说，维基百科避免了Facebook，Twitter和Youtube的一些陷阱，因为它不支持广告。这意味着该网站没有旨在提高互动度的目标算法。 ＆＃34;我们的动机是使人们能够找到所需的信息，＆＃34;他说。 ＆＃34;不要让它们停留在尽可能长的时间，以使它们呈现更多。
 因此，当大社会平台在选举后面临不和谐之时，维基百科却能够相对平静地放低自己。这也是一件好事。毫不奇怪，2020年美国总统大选页面是该年度网站中最受欢迎的页面之一。
 Wikipedia并没有成为打击虚假信息的工具，＆＃34;柴郡说。但这是巨大的社会利益。 
尊重他人，保持礼貌，保持话题。 我们会删除违反我们政策的评论，建议您阅读。 我们可以随时酌情关闭讨论线程。
title: 20岁那年，维基百科已成为Big Tech错误信息的避难所
title_en: At 20, Wikipedia has become a refuge from Big Tech's misinformation

note:
有时我们讲的最诱人的故事就是那些细节被遗忘的故事。物体和面部可能会在半光线下更漂亮。我们看到一个模糊的形状，然后添加所需的线条和阴影。我们听到一个故事的一部分，并添加我们希望可能是真实的另一部分。
 去年八月，我首先了解到了一个名为“几乎无害”的人。一位有线读者向我的提示发送了一条便条：一名徒步旅行者的尸体于2018年夏天在佛罗里达州的一个帐篷中被发现，但数十名业余侦探以及一些专业侦探也无法弄清楚他是谁是。每个人都知道他一年半前开始从纽约的阿巴拉契亚小径上向南走去。他在路上遇到了数百人，似乎使所有人都着迷。他告诉人们他来自路易斯安那州的巴吞鲁日，并且在纽约从事技术工作。他们都知道他的足迹名称，但是没人知道他的真实名字。
 我刚刚和三岁的12岁儿子在阿巴拉契亚小径上和从山下徒步旅行了三天，我被拉进来了。我们生活在一个不断进行机器监视和跟踪的时代。然而，某种程度上，大多数无害人已经逃脱了数字网络。他旅行时没有电话或身份证。他携带现金，无法通过信用卡收据进行追踪。通过面部识别软件运行时，他的指纹没有存储在任何数据库中，并且图像也没有显示任何结果。发现他尸体的佛罗里达州科利尔县的当局被绊倒了，但他们确定他死于自然原因。他一定很聪明。他似乎很友善。他以一般，熟悉的方式很帅。将一个温柔的故事描绘到他的过去很容易。
  他的生活充满了悲剧的神秘色彩。一个人独自死在黄色帐篷里，他的家人不知道。 “他一定很想念。北卡罗来纳州的一位女士娜塔莎·提斯利（Natasha Teasley）说，有人必须想念这个家伙。娜塔莎·提斯利（Natasha Teasley）组织了一个Facebook小组，数千人致力于发现他的身份。小组成员为他点燃了蜡烛。他们谈论“带他回家”。他们搜寻了每个失踪人员的数据库。每个人都有一个他们想要真实的故事：他正试图逃脱现代社会。他正试图逃避医学诊断。他试图逃脱一个想要伤害他的人。这是利用互联网做点好事的方法。
  我在总统大选的前一天发表了一篇文章，内容涉及“几乎无害”。超过一百五十万人阅读了这个故事，并看着其他远足者张贴的照片。人们向我发送了有关他可能成为谁或他可能正在做的事情的理论。他的腹部长了疤痕，读者被诊断出潜在的疾病。他的牙齿完美，这表明他小时候需要良好的牙齿保健。其他人则在《达芬奇密码》层面上寻找线索。他在旅馆前以“本·比利米”（Ben Bilemy）的身份登录，经过一些创造性的努力，它可以反过来读为“为什么我，lib？”有时他们只是让他们的想像力飞扬。 “我认为他可能是外星人，”一位读者写信给我。 “一种星际DeTocqueville漫长而漫长的旅行，以了解人与星球，当他完成后，他浪费了精力，回到了半人马座阿尔法（Alpha Centauri）。考虑一下。”
  而且，当然，人们认为他们知道他是谁。故事上线后的几个小时，我通过DM获得了我的第一个ID。 “嗨，这是一封令人发疯的短信，但我相信我知道那个徒步旅行者是谁。”我的通讯员和一个看起来像徒步旅行者，名字叫比利米的人一起上了高中。后来打了几个电话，很明显，线索是一条红鲱鱼。她的前同学还健在。
  提示不断出现。路易斯安那州的一位妇女给我寄了一张哥哥的照片，她的哥哥与失踪的男子有着千丝万缕的相似之处，并告诉我她怀疑“多数无害”是她的毒贩叔叔的私生子。一个男人被认为是远足者在新奥尔良的一个顽固的朋克摇滚乐队中演奏。但是到目前为止，最诱人的秘诀来自弗吉尼亚州的一个人，他简短地说服了我认识该名徒步旅行者，他的名字叫达里尔·麦肯齐。我的通讯员讲述了一个令人感动的故事，该故事是在纽波特纽斯保龄球馆与该人交往，并听说达里尔患有晚期癌症，并计划步行至他的死亡。达里尔曾说过：“我没有名字就进入了这个世界，我将没有名字就离开了这个世界。” 
我开始寻找细节以验证这个故事。我告诉我的编辑，她也很着迷，她在Facebook上找到了达里尔·麦肯齐（Daryl McKenzie）的一个页面，该页面自2017年（多数无害之旅开始之年）以来一直没有活跃。麦肯齐只有四个Facebook朋友，他唯一的帖子是旷野的照片。一定是他。我联系了一位朋友，并解释说一个徒步旅行者已经失踪了，他的名字可能是达里尔·麦肯齐。我已经写了有关他的故事并将其发布在网上。她哭了起来。 “哦，不，达里尔，”她的声音颤抖着说道。
 我感觉很糟糕。我想帮助确定失踪的远足者。但是我没有集中精力解决所有可能带来的痛苦。我告诉她，我很抱歉突然这么突发如此糟糕的消息。如果她愿意，她应该花点时间打电话给我。两分钟后，我的电话响了。她说：“那不是达里尔。”我故事中的照片看上去完全不像她的朋友，她的朋友确实是一位徒步旅行者，但在洛杉矶还活得很好。他从未在纽波特新闻打保龄球。
 同时，专心致志的Facebook猎人不断前进。他们很聪明。在旅途中，“几乎无害”（Mostly Harmless）随身携带了一个针对程序员的在线策略游戏Screeps的笔记本。因此，一个专注于数字取证的小组仔细研究了直到2017年4月（即大多数无害之日）开始远足的所有其他远足者的使用日期的所有可能使用者的帐户。他们在名为Vaejor的用户身上串珠。同时，一名名叫萨哈尔·比格德利（Sahar Bigdeli）的妇女安排该国一位主要的同位素分析家研究徒步旅行者的牙齿，希望能找到有关他的住处的线索。一家名为Othram的基因组学公司已经获取了他的DNA，并开始进行尖端的遗传分析以鉴定他的身份。科利尔县给他们寄了一块骨头碎片。他们已经提取了远足者的DNA，然后开始在名为GEDmatch的数据库中寻找人们之间的遗传相似性，以建立一棵潜在亲戚的树。他们了解到，徒步旅行者具有印第安根。他的家人来自路易斯安那的Assumption Parish；并有一个姓罗德里格斯（Rodriguez）的家庭成员。该公司的创始人戴维·米特尔曼（David Mittelman）在Facebook上谈论了此案。我在个人页面上购买了Facebook广告，以宣传我在路易斯安那州的故事，我认为他的亲戚可能住在那里。
  12月中旬，“几乎无害”的照片在巴吞鲁日的一群朋友中找到了路，其中一个人叫科利尔县警长办公室。这个朋友要求用她的中间名玛丽（Marie）来称呼，她告诉侦探她知道那个徒步旅行者是谁。治安官办公室收到了数百个坏消息。但是，这似乎是真实的。玛丽认出了她的脸，她对疤痕一无所知。笔迹很熟悉，编码风格也很熟悉。
 第二天早上5:30，我的电话响了。谁是八月首次发送小费的人。他说，我们有个名字：万斯·约翰·罗德里格斯。他给两张新照片发了短信，看上去像是“无害”。鼻子是一样的。耳朵。眼睛周围有黑眼圈。我在某种程度上兴高采烈。这个谜团似乎已经解决了。但是后来我回想起了与达里尔·麦肯齐（Daryl McKenzie）朋友的电话。有人现在必须告诉他的家人。有人必须告诉所有想念他的人。
 我开始伸出援手，首先是玛丽，然后是其他老朋友和女友。我和其他人努力确认他的身份，有关罗德里格斯的第一则新闻报道出现在12月下旬的《冒险杂志》上。当奥索姆（Othram）确认远足者的DNA与罗德里格斯（Rodriguez）的母亲的DNA相符时，这个难题今天已正式解决。
 我们所有人都在讲述自己的生平。但是，旅途在黄色帐篷中结束的那个人不是任何人想到或希望过的人。如果他一直想逃脱某件事，那就是他自己。 
万斯·约翰·罗德里格斯（Vance John Rodriguez），1976年2月生于巴吞鲁日（Baton Rouge）附近。他有一个双胞胎姐姐和一个哥哥。多年来，他告诉朋友说，父亲深深地伤害了他，但我与之交谈的人似乎都不知道该如何做。据朋友说，罗德里格斯（Rodriguez）大约15岁时，拿着枪前往野外，打算自杀。他开胃了。但是后来，当他流血致死时，他决定活下去。他无力地举起了手，一辆过往的卡车看见了他，将车停了下来。随之而来的手术是疤痕的原因，这一疤痕引起了Facebook团体的极大兴趣。后来，他告诉朋友他想被埋在那个领域。
 罗德里格斯（Rodriguez）在17岁时得到父母的同意，在路易斯安那州的拉斐特（Lafayette）法院获得了解放。玛丽（Marie）在他二十多岁的时候与他一起住了好几年，他说，他对自己的父母在自杀后将他寄养给他的父母感到很生气。玛丽回忆说：“除了说'干他们'，他不会谈论父母。”一月初，即他们听到这个消息两个星期后，我给他的父母和姐姐写信。他的妹妹回信说：“我的家人无可奉告。”
 高中毕业后，罗德里格斯（Rodriguez）就读于路易斯安那西南大学，现为路易斯安那大学拉斐特分校。在学校的计算机实验室中，他认识了一个叫Randall Godso的人。在接下来的五年中，他们成为了断断续续的室友。有时他们会出去参加聚会；罗德里格斯（Rodriguez）的一位朋友写道，她记得他来宿舍并在钢琴上弹奏Metallica的《别无所求》。她写道：“我可以在他周围保持安静，而且从来没有感到尴尬。”
 Godso和Rodriguez都是计算机迷，而Rodriguez则将其推向了极致。 Godso记得他的室友每天玩18个小时，把其他所有东西都拒之门外。 “他将经历巨大的抑郁症发作。他去了一年，没有面带微笑或对人不友善。”戈德索回忆道。据他的室友说，罗德里格斯（Rodriguez）切断了与家人的所有联系。 “他一生都很沮丧，情绪低落，”戈德索回忆道。 “但是我需要一个室友，我们相处得很好。” Godso补充说，他不记得Rodriguez曾经对在野外消磨时光表现出任何兴趣。 “外面是在汽车和建筑物之间。”
  根据Godso的说法，Rodriguez没有毕业。但是具有计算机技能的人通常不会很难找到工作。最终，他开始在巴吞鲁日的一家名为Shoppers Choice的电子商务公司工作，在那里他被许多人公认为团队中最有才华的工程师。罗德里格斯（Rodriguez）编写的代码在该公司的代码库中仍然充满“ VR”符号。从事IT工作的Marie告诉我：“他是个疯狂的好程序员。除非他总是以最困难的方式编写所有代码，就像您雇用伦勃朗为您的浴室粉刷一样。您知道它会被点亮，但上方。”
  他并不是特别合作，但是他会坐下来，戴上耳机-听狗神殿和机器发怒，并解决问题。随着问题变得越来越复杂，他变得更加自在。他很安静，但对他的同事却不是这样。一位前同事科里·蒂斯代尔（Corey Tisdale）说：“如果你问我，他是否是穿着小丑服从大炮中炸出东西的那个人，那不是他。” “但是他会去度假聚会，看起来并不痛苦。”
 他每天吃一次，经常是沃尔玛（Walmart）的披萨或Pasta Kitchen（意大利面食）的千层面。他穿着黑色牛仔裤，黑色衬衫和黑色风衣。他的长长的黑发几乎垂到了腰间。有一天，他把所有的东西都割掉了，交给了爱之锁。他参加了Dragon Con。他似乎患有一些心理健康问题，但据玛丽说，他拒绝了传统医学。她说：“他以药物和饮酒为自疗对象。”他会继续进行玛丽和其他朋友所说的“停电”，在那里他连续几天不动，拒绝食物和所有人的接触。但最终他会从中脱颖而出。玛丽回忆说：“他的悲伤像一层多余的皮肤一样。”但是，她补充说：“我确实挖掘出了他不完美的完美单身自我。” 
在巴吞鲁日的这段时间里，罗德里格斯开始了一段长达五年的恋爱关系。但是结局非常糟糕。约会结束后，他约会的那个女人在她的Facebook页面上写道：“每月950欧元的公寓/每月300欧元的费用/站起来，在5年内打动你的肉体和情感？无价。”罗德里格斯（Rodriguez）被确定为远足者后，该名女子的母亲在Facebook上发表评论：“这个人对我的女儿如此虐待，于是改变了她。”
  从那时起，他的同事们，现在得知他的故事，似乎感到难过。但并不完全感到惊讶。他总是内向，内向。他的笑话通常晦涩难懂，”一位名叫Keith Parent的同事说。 “除了最后他死了的事实，这一切都不令人惊讶。”
 “我在2017年中寻找万斯，以聘请他为我的客户开发应用程序，”购物者选择协会的另一名同事大卫·布拉泽尔（David Blazier）说。 “而且我实际上会付给他他所要求的任何东西。我没找到他。”
 2013年，罗德里格斯移居纽约市。他在网上聊天室认识了一个女人，我称她为K。由于公众对徒步旅行者的痴迷而要求匿名的K当时正在纽约州北部完成大学。他们来回走访。随着关系的发展，他们决定搬到纽约市并共同生活。她正在流行时尚，必须去那里。他在路易斯安那州度过了自己的一生，并欢迎这一改变。他以前从未见过雪。起初，他浪漫而可爱。但是很快他就开始鼓掌并把她拒之门外。 “如果有什么让他不高兴的事，他将完全停止跟我说话。当您共享一个500平方英尺的公寓时，这可能很孤单。”她说。
  罗德里格斯（Rodriguez）为“购物者选择”（Shoppers Choice）远程工作了大约一年，然后辞职并以积蓄为生。她回忆说，他和K可能每个月出去一次。她会问他是否想旅行，他会回答说他不需要去任何地方，因为他可以轻松地在网上看照片。这座城市充满了不断的运动，但这似乎使他陷入了紧张状态。 K回忆说：“我认为，在一个有这么多人并且没有人可与之联系的地方，他变得更加孤独。”
 枯燥的关系逐渐恶化。 K回忆说：“他确实向我公开了他所认识的以前的女性以及他如何对待她们。他们应该是危险信号。”尽管她不祥，但​​她还是和他在一起。 “有一次，我洗完澡没穿衣服，他把我锁在公寓外面，因为我们开始争论一些我什至不记得的事情。那不是他唯一一次把我拒之门外。”
 2016年9月的一个星期六晚上，一名恐怖分子在曼哈顿西23街引爆炸弹，炸伤了K。 “我的PTSD非常糟糕，他讨厌照顾我，甚至每次我需要帮助时都会留下过时的日志，以至于他在黑暗中把我抛在了外面-知道那时我不能她补充道，在回忆之前，她回忆道，“独自一人在外面还是在黑暗中不要惊慌，这只是轻巧的东西。” 
根据K的说法，大约在这个时候，罗德里格斯（Rodriguez）也提出了一个威胁，既威胁他的技能，又讽刺，因为他将要寻求的匿名性：他威胁说，如果K离开他，他会威胁K。那个冬天她仍然搬出去。他联系了Godso，他记得担心罗德里格斯（Rodriguez）自杀。 2017年1月，罗德里格斯（Rodriguez）在Screeps用户的Slack频道中写道：“（目前）我几乎无害。” 4月中旬，他在最后一刻的Screeps Slack中张贴了他的最后一条消息，然后进入树林。他似乎匆匆离开了。八个月后，当房东打开公寓的门时，他发现未打开的食物以及罗德里格斯的护照，钱包和信用卡。
 罗德里格斯（Rodriguez）在接下来的15个月里向南远足，并撤走了他曾经留下的那个男人的所有残余物。据在路上看到他照片的朋友说，他看上去比以前更健康。他在微笑。每个人都喜欢他。他变得不同了吗？我问了这个问题。 “当您初次见到他时，他很风度翩翩，但在与他共度更多时间之后，他的性格彻底改变了。路上的人们没有和他在一起多年，以了解他如何处理跌宕起伏。她说：“也许他擅长代码转换和隐藏与我或其他人一起在幕后的人。” “我认为他有能力与完全陌生的人成为这个人真是太痛了，但是当涉及到我们时，他甚至不能成为一个体面的人，以任何尊严对待我或我的身体。”
  当他沿着阿巴拉契亚山脉步道行驶时，万斯·罗德里格斯不受义务约束，并且从科技时代开始就被现金充裕。而且没有人在找他。他的家人没有联系。他的前女友很怕他。就像玛丽说的那样，他在路易斯安那州的朋友们以为他陷入了“大麻烦”。她说：“万斯切断了所有联系，离开了。” “每个人都认为他会出现。”
  当我在11月写有关神秘的徒步旅行者的文章时，我以两个问题结束了这个故事：“为什么大多数无害的人走进树林？为什么当事情开始出错时，他不走出来吗？”
 罗德里格斯（Rodriguez）的朋友对第二个问题有一个理论。他最后几个月的时间表尚不清楚，但他似乎被困住并挨饿，也许是在他于2018年7月23日被发现的同一个露营地。当两个徒步旅行者偶然跌倒在他的帐篷上时，他的体重只有83磅。不过，他有钱，而且距主要高速公路只有几英里。也许他的经验不足吸引了他，但虫子，蛇和潮湿使他无与伦比。他的朋友建议，他最后一次重大停电的可能性更大。 K告诉我：“我知道当他不得不处理任何事情时，他都会躺下睡觉。” “我感觉就是这样。他会无视问题，而“睡着直到它消失。””
 另一个问题更难：他为什么要先进入树林？有一个简单的（如果是简略的）答案，可能适用于那些思维横冲直撞的人。我们走出去是因为它有助于我们进入自己的内心。我们站在树上，呼吸着雪松的气味，我们可以思考和感受。我们的电话不会响，我们的屏幕也不会招手。我们站在大自然的怀抱中，记住我们有多小，一切都变慢了。
  当我试图理解罗德里格斯时，我想到了一个我认识的名叫杰西·科迪的人，他在高中越野比赛中与之比赛。像罗德里格斯（Rodriguez）一样，科迪（Cody）在二十多岁和三十多岁的时候都在挣扎。他对妇女的待遇很差。他变得不喜欢自己。他曾考虑过自杀。然后，在一个顿悟中，他决定徒步阿巴拉契亚小径，尽管之前从未搭过帐篷。在树林里，他找到了驯服恶魔的方法。他现在经营一个组织，以帮助陷入困境的人们融入大自然。从那以后他一直没有停止远足。 
罗德里格斯（Rodriguez）的故事也许和科迪（Cody）的故事相似。 他独自在一个陌生的广阔城市里。 他破坏了他的关系。 他生气地离开了他的公寓。 然后，当他穿越山脉时，  ......
note_en:
Sometimes the most alluring stories we tell are the ones with the details left out. Objects and faces can be prettier in the half light. We see a faint shape and we add the lines and shadows we want. We hear one part of a story and add another part that we hope might be true.
 I first learned of the man called Mostly Harmless this past August. A WIRED reader sent a note to my  tip line: The body of a hiker had been found in a tent in Florida in the summer of 2018, but scores of amateur detectives, and a few professional ones too, couldn’t figure out who he was. Everyone knew that he had started walking south on the Appalachian Trail from New York a year and a half before. He met hundreds of people on the trail, and seemed to charm them all. He told people he was from Baton Rouge, Louisiana, and that he worked in tech in New York. They all knew his trail name, but no one could figure out his real one.
 I had just spent three days hiking on and off the Appalachian Trail with my 12-year-old son, and I was pulled in. We live in an age of constant machine surveillance and tracking. Yet somehow Mostly Harmless had escaped the digital dragnet. He had traveled without a phone or an ID. He carried cash and couldn’t be tracked by credit card receipts. His fingerprints weren’t in any database and his image didn’t turn up any results when run through facial recognition software. The authorities in Collier County, Florida, where his body was found, were stumped, but they were certain he had died of natural causes. He must have been smart. He appeared to have been kind. He was handsome in a general, familiar kind of way. It was easy to map a gentle story onto his past.
  His life was a mystery packed inside a tragedy. A man had died alone in a yellow tent, and his family didn’t know. “He’s got to be missed. Someone must miss this guy,” said Natasha Teasley, a woman in North Carolina who organized a Facebook group with several thousand people dedicated to discovering his identity. Members of the group lit candles for him. They talked about “bringing him home.” They scoured every missing-persons database. Everyone had a story they wanted to be true: He was trying to escape modern society. He was trying to escape a medical diagnosis. He was trying to escape someone who wanted to hurt him. This was a way to use the internet to do something good.
  I published  an article about Mostly Harmless the day before the presidential election. More than one and a half million people read the story and looked at photos that other hikers had posted. People sent me theories about who he could have been or what he might have been doing. He had a long scar on his abdomen and readers diagnosed potential illnesses. He had perfect teeth, which suggested good dental care as a child. Others dug into  Da Vinci Code–level clues. He had signed in at hostels as “Ben Bilemy,” which, with some creative effort, could be read in reverse as “Why me, lib?” And sometimes they just let their imaginations fly. “I think he could be a space alien,” one reader wrote to me. “A kind of astral DeTocqueville taking a long, long trip to get a sense of the people and the planet, and when he was done, he wasted away and went back to Alpha Centauri. Think about it.”
  And, of course, people thought they knew who he was. A few hours after the story went live, I got my first ID via DM. “Hi, this is a crazy note to be sending but I believe I know who the hiker was.” My correspondent had gone to high school with someone who looked like the hiker and whose name was something like Bilemy. A few phone calls later and it was clear the lead was a red herring. Her former classmate was alive and well.
  The tips kept coming in. One Louisiana woman sent me a photograph of her brother, who bore an uncanny resemblance to the missing man, and told me she suspected Mostly Harmless was the illegitimate son of her drug-dealing uncle. A man was convinced the hiker had played in a hardcore punk rock band in New Orleans. But by far the most enticing tip came from a man in Virginia who persuaded me, briefly, that he had known the hiker and that his name was Daryl McKenzie. My correspondent told a moving story of befriending the man in a Newport News bowling alley and hearing that Daryl had terminal cancer and planned to hike to his death. Daryl had supposedly said, “I came into this world without a name and I’m going to go out of this world without one.”
 I began searching for details to validate the tale. I told  my editor, who got obsessed too, and she found a Facebook page for a Daryl McKenzie that hadn’t been active since 2017, the year Mostly Harmless started his trek. McKenzie had just four Facebook friends and his only posts were photos of the wilderness. It had to be him. I contacted one of the friends and explained that a hiker had disappeared and that his name might have been Daryl McKenzie. I’d written about his story and posted it online. She burst into tears. “Oh, no, Daryl,” she said as her voice quavered.
 I felt awful. I’d wanted to help identify the missing hiker. But I hadn’t focused on all the pain that could bring. I told her that I was sorry to have broken such terrible news so suddenly. She should take her time and call me back whenever, if she even wanted to. Two minutes later my phone rang. “That’s not Daryl,” she said. The photos in my story didn’t look at all like her friend, who was indeed a hiker but who was alive and well in Los Angeles. He had never been bowling in Newport News.
 Meanwhile, the dedicated Facebook hunters kept going. And they were ingenious. On the trail, Mostly Harmless had carried a notebook full of ideas for  Screeps, an online strategy game for programmers. And so a group focused on digital forensics went through the accounts of every possible user who had been on  Screeps up until April 2017, the date Mostly Harmless had given other hikers for when he’d begun his journey. They had a bead on a user named  Vaejor. Meanwhile, a woman named Sahar Bigdeli had arranged for one of the country’s leading isotope analysts to study the hiker’s teeth in hopes that clues could be discovered about where he had lived. A genomics company, Othram, had taken his DNA and started to do  cutting-edge genetic analysis to identify him. Collier County had sent them a bone fragment; they had extracted the hiker’s DNA and then begun searching for genetic similarities among people in a database called GEDmatch to build a tree of potential relatives. They learned that the hiker had Cajun roots; that his family had come from Assumption Parish, Louisiana; and that there were family members with the name Rodriguez. The founder of the company, David Mittelman, went on Facebook to  talk about the case. I bought Facebook ads on  my personal page to promote my story in the region of Louisiana where I thought his relatives likely lived.
  In the middle of December, photographs of Mostly Harmless found their way to a group of friends in Baton Rouge, one of whom called the Collier County Sheriff’s Office. This friend, who asked to be referred to by her middle name, Marie, told the detective that she knew who the hiker was. The sheriff’s office had received hundreds of bad tips. But this one seemed real. Marie recognized the face and she knew all about the scar. The handwriting was familiar, and the coding style too.
 At 5:30 the next morning, my phone rang. It was the same person who had first sent the tip in August. We have a name, he said: Vance John Rodriguez. He texted two new photographs that looked just like Mostly Harmless. The nose was the same. The ears. The eyes with dark circles around them. I was elated to some degree. The mystery appeared to be solved. But then I thought back to my phone call to the friend of Daryl McKenzie. Someone was going to have to tell his family now. Someone would have to tell all the people who missed him.
 I started reaching out, first to Marie, then to other old friends and girlfriends. I and others worked to confirm his identity, with the first  press story about Rodriguez appearing in late December in  Adventure Journal. The puzzle was formally solved today, when  Othram confirmed that the DNA of the hiker matched that of Rodriguez’s mother.
 We'd all been telling ourselves stories about his life. But the man whose journey had ended in the yellow tent wasn’t who anyone thought or hoped. If he had been trying to escape something, it was himself.
  Vance John Rodriguez, a k a Vaejor, was born in February 1976 near Baton Rouge. He had a twin sister and an older brother. He told friends over the years that his father had deeply hurt him, but no one I spoke with seems to be clear exactly how. When he was about 15, according to friends, Rodriguez headed off into a field with a gun, intending to kill himself. He fired into his stomach. But then, as he lay bleeding to death, he decided to live. He raised his hand weakly and a passing truck saw him and pulled over. The surgeries that followed were the cause of the scar that had so intrigued the Facebook group. Later, he would tell friends that he wanted to be buried in that field.
 At 17, with the consent of his parents, Rodriguez was emancipated by a Lafayette, Louisiana, court. Marie, who lived with him as a friend for several years in his twenties, says he was angry that his parents had institutionalized him after the near suicide. “He would not talk about his parents except to say ‘fuck them,’” Marie recalls. I wrote to his parents and sister in early January, two weeks after they heard the news. His sister wrote back, “My family has no comment.”
 After graduating from high school, Rodriguez  enrolled at the University of Southwestern Louisiana, now the University of Louisiana at Lafayette. In the school’s computer lab, he came to know a man named Randall Godso. They became off-and-on roommates for the next five years. Occasionally they would go out and party; one friend of Rodriguez’s wrote that she remembers him coming to her dorm and playing “Nothing Else Matters” by Metallica on the piano. “I could be quiet around him,” she wrote, “and it never felt awkward.”
 Godso and Rodriguez were both computer nerds, with Rodriguez taking it to the extreme. Godso remembers his roommate playing games for 18 hours a day and shutting everything else out. “He would go through huge bouts of depression. He’d go for a year without smiling or being nice to people,” Godso recalls. Rodriguez, according to his roommate, had cut off all contact with his family. “He was depressed and moody his whole life,” Godso recalls. “But I needed a roommate and we got along OK.” Godso adds that he doesn’t remember Rodriguez ever showing any interest in spending time in the wild. “Outside was between the car and the building.”
  According to Godso, Rodriguez didn’t graduate. But people with computer skills usually don’t have a hard time finding a job. Eventually, he started work at an ecommerce company based in Baton Rouge called Shoppers Choice, where he was recognized by many as the most talented engineer on the team. The company’s codebase is still filled with notations of “VR,” for code that Rodriguez wrote. Marie, who works in IT, told me, “He was a crazy good coder. Except he would always code everything the hardest way possible, kind of like you hired Rembrandt to paint your bathroom. You know it is going to be lit, but over the top.”
  He wasn’t particularly collaborative, but he would sit down, put on his headphones— listening to Temple of the Dog and Rage Against the Machine—and solve problems. As the problems got more complex, he got more comfortable. He was quiet but not, to his coworkers, perversely so. “If you’re asking me if he is the guy who shows up at the party in a clown suit blasting things out of a cannon, that’s not him,” says a former colleague named Corey Tisdale. “But he would go to holiday parties and not look miserable.”
 He ate once a day, often pizza from Walmart or lasagna from Pasta Kitchen. He wore black jeans, a black shirt, and a black trench coat. He had long, dark hair almost down to his waist. One day he cut it all off and gave it to Locks of Love. He  attended  Dragon Con. He appeared to suffer from some mental health issues, but, according to Marie, he refused conventional medicine. “He self-medicated with drinking and chocolate,” she says. He would go on what Marie and other friends called “outages,” where he lay immobile for days, refusing food and all human contact. But eventually he would snap out of it. “He wore his sadness like an extra layer of skin,” Marie recalls. But, she adds, “I truly dug his imperfectly perfect solitary singular self.”
 During this time in Baton Rouge Rodriguez started a relationship that would last for five years. But it ended quite badly. When it was over, the woman he had dated wrote on her Facebook page, “Apartment 950 a month / bills 300 a month / Standing up to the monster that beat you up emotionally and physically for 5 years? Priceless.” After Rodriguez was identified as the hiker, the woman’s mother commented on Facebook, “This man was so abusive to my daughter, he changed her.”
  His colleagues from that time, learning of his story now, seemed saddened. But not entirely surprised. “He was always very introverted, kept to himself. His jokes were usually obscure,” says a colleague named Keith Parent. “None of this is surprising, except for the fact that, in the end, he died.”
 “I looked for Vance in mid-2017 to hire him to build an app for a client of mine,” says another coworker from Shoppers Choice named David Blazier. “And I would have paid him literally anything he asked. I never found him.”
 In 2013, Rodriguez moved to New York City. He’d met a woman, whom I’ll call K, in an online chat room. K, who asked for anonymity because of the public obsession about the hiker, was then finishing college in upstate New York. They traveled back and forth to visit each other. As their relationship evolved, they decided to both move to New York City and live together. She was going into fashion and had to be there. He had spent his life in Louisiana and welcomed the change. He’d never seen snow before. At first, he was romantic and sweet. But soon he started to clam up and shut her out. “If something upset him, he would stop talking to me completely. Which can be lonely when you share a 500-square-foot apartment,” she says.
  Rodriguez kept working remotely for Shoppers Choice for about a year, then quit and lived off his savings. He and K went out maybe once a month, she recalls. She would ask him if he wanted to travel, and he would respond that he didn’t need to go anywhere because he could easily look at pictures online. The city was filled with constant motion, but that seemed to render him catatonic. “I think it made him even more lonely to be in a place with so many people and no one to connect to,” K recalls.
 Gradually, the dreary relationship got worse. K recalls, “He did open up to me about previous women that he knew and how he treated them. They should have been red flags.” She stayed with him, despite her foreboding. “At one point he locked me out of our apartment after I got out of the shower without clothing because we started arguing about something I can't even remember. That wasn't the only time he locked me out.”
 On a Saturday night in September 2016, K was injured when a terrorist  set off a bomb on West 23rd Street in Manhattan. “I had pretty bad PTSD to which he hated caring for me, even kept a dated log of every time I needed help, to the point where he left me outside in the dark—knowing that at that time I couldn't be outside alone or be in the dark without panicking,” she recalls, before adding, “and this is only the light stuff.”
 Around this time, according to K, Rodriguez also made a threat that was both terrifying because of his skills and ironic because of the anonymity he was about to seek: He threatened to dox K if she ever left him. She still moved out that winter. He reached out to Godso, who remembers worrying that Rodriguez would commit suicide. In January 2017, Rodriguez wrote,  in a Slack channel for  Screeps users, “I'm mostly harmless (for now).” In mid-April, he posted his last message in the  Screeps Slack and headed into the woods. He seems to have left in a hurry. When his landlord opened the door to the apartment, eight months later,  he found unopened food along with Rodriguez’s passport, wallet, and credit cards.
 Rodriguez spent the next 15 months hiking south and shedding all remnants of the man he’d been. According to friends who saw the photographs of him on the trail, he looked healthier than ever. He was smiling. Everyone liked him. Had he become a different person? I asked K this question. “He was personable when you first met him, but after spending more time with him in an intimate way his personality completely changed. The people on the trail didn't spend years with him to see how he handled ups and downs. Maybe he was good at code-switching and hiding the person he was behind doors with me or others,” she said. “I think it just hurts that he was capable of being this person with complete strangers, but when it came to us he couldn't even be a decent human being to treat me or my body with any dignity.”
  As he traveled down the Appalachian Trail, Vance Rodriguez was unencumbered by obligations and flush with cash from his time in tech. And there wasn’t anyone looking for him. His family wasn’t in touch. His ex-girlfriend was afraid of him. And his friends in Louisiana just thought he was in “a long-ass outage,” as Marie puts it. “Vance cut all ties and left,” she says. “Everyone assumed he would show back up.”
  When I wrote about the mysterious hiker in November, I ended the story with two questions: “Why did Mostly Harmless walk into the woods? And why, when things started to go wrong, didn’t he walk out?”
 Rodriguez’s friends have a theory about the second question. The timeline of his last few months is unclear, but he appears to have been stuck and starving, maybe at the same campground where he was found on July 23, 2018. By the time two hikers stumbled upon his tent, his body weighed just 83 pounds. He had money, though, and he was just a few miles from a major highway. Maybe his inexperience caught up to him and he was outmatched by the bugs, the snakes, and the humidity. It’s more likely, his friends suggest, that he had one last, major outage.  “I know that when he had to deal with anything, he would just lay down and sleep,”  K told me. “I feel like that's what happened. He would ignore problems and ‘sleep until it was gone.’”
 The other question is harder: Why did he go into the woods to begin with? There is a simple, if reductive, answer that might apply to anyone whose mind is going sideways. We go outside because it helps take us inside ourselves. We stand in the trees, breathe in the scent of cedar, and we can think and feel. Our phones don’t ring and our screens don’t beckon us. We stand in the vastness of nature, remember how small we are, and everything slows down.
  As I tried to make sense of Rodriguez, I thought about a man I know named  Jesse Cody who I had raced against in high school cross country. Like Rodriguez, Cody had struggled in his twenties and thirties. He had treated women poorly. He had come to dislike himself. He had contemplated suicide. Then, in an epiphany, he had decided to hike the Appalachian Trail, despite never having pitched a tent before. And there, in the woods, he figured out how to tame his demons. He now runs an organization to help people struggling with depression by taking them into nature. And he hasn’t stopped hiking since.
 Maybe Rodriguez’s story is similar to Cody’s. He was alone in a vast, unfamiliar city. He’d destroyed his relationships. He left his apartment in anger. And then, as he traversed the mountains,

 此版本已彻底清除了工件，并压缩为易于转换为电子书友好格式的内容。您可以在GitHub上在线阅读此版本，或下载任何EPUB或Mobi版本。您可以克隆存储库，并在必要时使用pandoc生成自己的版本。

note_en:
This is the source for an ebook version of Michael Abrash's Black Book of Graphics Programming (Special Edition), originally published in 1997 and  released online for free in 2001.
  The  GitHub releases list has an EPUB and Mobi version available for download, and you can find a mirror of the HTML version at  www.jagregory.com/abrash-black-book.
  The book is now out of print, and hard to come by. Last time I checked, it was going for over $200 on eBay.
 The version which Michael and Dr. Dobbs released in 2001 was a collection of PDF files. That version is  still available. However, the structure (multiple files) and the format (PDF) result in a poor user experience on an ebook reader or other mobile device.
 This version has been thoroughly cleaned of artifacts and condensed into something which can easily be converted into an ebook-friendly format. You can read this version online at GitHub, or download any of the EPUB or Mobi releases. You can clone the repository and generate your own version with  pandoc if necessary.
  Changes are welcome, especially conversion-related ones. If you spot any problems while reading, please  submit an issue and I'll correct it. Pull requests are always welcome.
 Some larger changes could be made to improve the content. I'd love to see some of the images converted to a vector representation so we can provide higher-resolution versions. Formulas and equations could be typeset with  MathJax.
  You need to have the following software installed and on your  PATH before you begin:
  To generate an e-reader friendly version of the book, you can use  make with one of the following options:
  Once complete, there will be an  out directory with a  black-book.epub, a  black-book.mobi and an  html directory with a  black-book.html file.
 Note: Generating a Mobi requires an EPUB to already exist. Also, Mobi generation can be  slow because of compression. If you want a quick Mobi conversion you can just run  kindlegen out/black-book.epub.

note:
考古学家发现了世界上最古老的洞穴壁画：一幅真人大小的野猪照片，至少在45500年前在印度尼西亚制作。周三在《科学进展》杂志上描述的发现提供了人类在该地区定居的最早证据。
  澳大利亚格里菲斯大学的合著者马克西姆·奥伯特（Maxime Aubert）告诉法新社，博士生巴斯朗·布尔汉（Basran Burhan）于2017年在苏拉威西岛上发现了该物种，这是该团队与印度尼西亚当局进行的调查的一部分。
  Leang Tedongnge洞穴位于一个偏远的山谷中，该山谷被纯粹的石灰岩峭壁包围，距离最近的道路约有一个小时的步行路程。
  由于在雨季洪水泛滥，所以只能在旱季才能进入。孤立的武吉士社区成员告诉团队，西方人从未见过。
  苏拉威西疣猪的尺寸为136 x 54厘米（53 x 21英寸），是用深红色的pigment石颜料涂成的，有一顶短直立的头发，以及一对成年雄性特征的角状面部疣。
  这只猪的后躯上方有两个手印，作为叙事场景的一部分，它似乎正面临着另外两只只被部分保存的猪。
   ＆＃34;这只猪似乎正在战斗或在另外两只疣猪之间，＆＃34;合著者亚当·布鲁姆（Adam Brumm）说。 
人类狩猎苏拉威西疣猪已有数万年之久，它们是该地区史前艺术品的重要特征，尤其是在冰河时期。
    约会专家奥伯特（Aubert）确定了在画上形成的方解石矿床，然后使用铀系列同位素测年法自信地说该矿床有45,500年的历史。
  这样可以使绘画至少达到该年龄，但它可能会更旧，因为我们仅使用的约会只在方解石上面加上了方解石的日期，＆＃34;他解释。
  ＆＃34;制作它的人是完全现代的，就像我们一样，他们拥有完成任何喜欢的绘画的全部能力和工具，＆＃34;他加了。
   苏拉威西岛的同一支队伍发现了以前最古老的岩石艺术品。它描绘了一群捕猎哺乳动物的部分人类和部分动物的雕像，被发现至少有43900岁。
  这样的洞穴壁画也有助于填补我们对早期人类迁徙的理解的空白。
  众所周知，人们在65,000年前就到达了澳大利亚，但他们可能不得不穿越被称为“华莱士”的印度尼西亚群岛。 
现在该网站代表了华莱士州人类的最古老证据，但它希望进一步的研究将有助于显示人们早在该地区，这将解决澳大利亚定居难题。  研究小组认为，该艺术品是由智人制成的，与现在已灭绝的人类（例如丹尼索瓦斯）相反，但不能肯定地说。  要制作手印，艺术家必须将手放在表面上，然后在上面吐出颜料，该团队希望从残留的唾液中提取DNA样本。 更多信息：A. Brumm el等人，＆＃34;在苏拉威西岛发现的最古老的洞穴艺术品，＆＃34; 科学进展（2020）。 advances.sciencemag.org/lookup….1126 / sciadv.abd4648
note_en:
Archaeologists have discovered the world's oldest known cave painting: a life-sized picture of a wild pig that was made at least 45,500 years ago in Indonesia.    The finding described in the journal  Science Advances on Wednesday provides the earliest evidence of human settlement of the region.
  Co-author Maxime Aubert of Australia's Griffith University told AFP it was found on the island of Sulawesi in 2017 by doctoral student Basran Burhan, as part of surveys the team was carrying out with Indonesian authorities.
  The Leang Tedongnge cave is located in a remote valley enclosed by sheer limestone cliffs, about an hour's walk from the nearest road.
  It is only accessible during the dry season because of flooding during the wet season—and members of the isolated Bugis community told the team it had never before been seen by Westerners.
  Measuring 136 by 54 centimeters (53 by 21 inches) the Sulawesi warty pig was painted using dark red ochre pigment and has a short crest of upright hair, as well as a pair of horn-like facial warts characteristic of adult males of the species.
  There are two hand prints above the pig's hindquarters, and it appears to be facing two other pigs that are only partially preserved, as part of a narrative scene.
   "The pig appears to be observing a fight or   between two other warty pigs," said co-author Adam Brumm.
  Humans have hunted Sulawesi warty pigs for tens of thousands of years, and they are a key feature of the region's prehistoric artwork, particularly during the Ice Age.
    Aubert, a dating specialist, identified a calcite deposit that had formed on top of the painting, then used Uranium-series isotope dating to confidently say the deposit was 45,500 years old.
  This makes the painting at least that age, "but it could be much older because the dating that we're using only dates the calcite on top of it," he explained.
  "The people who made it were fully modern, they were just like us, they had all of the capacity and the tools to do any painting that they liked," he added.
   The previously oldest dated rock art   was found by the same team in Sulawesi. It depicted a group of part-human, part-animal figures hunting mammals, and was found to be at least 43,900 years old.
  Cave paintings such as these also help fill in gaps about our understanding of early human migrations.
  It's known that people reached Australia 65,000 years ago, but they would probably have had to cross the islands of Indonesia, known as "Wallacea."
  This site now represents the oldest evidence of humans in Wallacea, but it's hoped further research will help show people were in the region much earlier, which would resolve the Australia settlement puzzle.
   The team believes the artwork was made by Homo sapiens, as opposed to now extinct human species like Denisovans, but cannot say this for certain.
  To make handprints, the artists would have had to place their hands on a surface then spit pigment over it, and the team are hoping to try to extract DNA samples from residual saliva.        More information:												A. Brumm el al., "Oldest cave art found in Sulawesi,"  Science Advances (2020).  advances.sciencemag.org/lookup … .1126/sciadv.abd4648
title: 在印度尼西亚发现的最古老的洞穴壁画
title_en: Oldest known cave painting found in Indonesia

自从我在80年代中期开始学习编码以来，一直跟随着我的应用程序类型，那就是控制台应用程序。多年以来，它们看起来一直都是Main（string [] args）和一些幼稚的不一致的命令行解析器。随着各种OSS帮助程序库的采用，这种情况逐渐得到改善。在本文中，我将逐步介绍今天是dotnet新控制台的替代起点，该方法可以大大减少日志记录，解析和参数验证所需的样板代码，让我专注于解决的问题而不是管道。
  搭建新项目的便捷方法是使用.NET SDK CLI的模板功能，它预装了一些模板，例如console，classlib等，但是除此之外，还可以创建自己的模板，为方便起见，我为您提供了便利，因此，安装了.NET 5 SDK后，您便可以轻松尝试看一下本文中讨论的所有内容。
  因此，根据我认为正确的配方，我们开始创建一个新的控制台应用程序。.NETSDK模板以NuGet包的形式分发，NuGet包的规范来源是NuGet.org，我已在其中发布了我的模板作为Devlead.Console.Template。使用dotnet new命令和--install packageId参数安装模板，在这种情况下：
   在本地安装模板之后，我们现在可以使用新的devleadconsole模板，根据我，基本依赖项和样板代码创建新的控制台应用程序：
   MyConsoleApp│MyConsoleApp.csproj│Program.cs│└───命令│ConsoleCommand.cs│├───Settings│ConsoleSettings.cs│└───验证ValidateStringAttribute.cs

       Nullable与值enable一起启用，启用C＃8中引入的可为空的引用类型功能，默认情况下使引用类型为不可为空，基本上将许多错误从运行时较晚捕获到编译时早期捕获。 
值为true的TreatWarningsAsErrors使编译器变得更加笨拙，它不仅会破坏编译器错误的构建，还会破坏编译器的警告，并与Nullable结合使用，我个人认为代码质量从一开始就会变得更好。
  Spectre.Console有很多东西，对于从事控制台应用程序的任何人来说都是一支真正的瑞士军队，但是在此模板中，它最重要的是一个非常固执的命令行解析器。
 Spectre.Cli.Extensions.DependencyInjection使Spectre.Console易于与标准Microsoft DependencyInjection互操作，默认情况下与用于ASP.NET和Azure函数的依赖项注入相同。
 简化的Microsoft.Extensions.Logging提供了标准的日志记录抽象，Microsoft.Extensions.Logging.Console提供了用于记录到控制台的实现。
 Microsoft.SourceLink.GitHub可以在工件和源代码控制之间进行自动跟踪，从而提供更好的调试和可跟踪性体验。
  生成的Program.cs使用新的C＃9顶级语句模式从应用程序中删除了不必要的仪式代码，但它包含的内容：
  注意：顶层语句表示随着RunAsync返回Task＆lt; int＆gt;，。NET 5会自动生成＆＃34;程序＆＃34;类和异步Task＆lt; int＆gt; Main（string args）为您服务，无需编写大量样板代码。 
使用Microsoft.Extensions.DependencyInjection;使用Microsoft.Extensions.Logging;使用Devlead.Console.Commands;使用Spectre.Console.Cli;使用Spectre.Cli.Extensions.DependencyInjection; var serviceCollection = new ServiceCollection（）.AddLogging（configure =＆gt; ;配置.AddSimpleConsole（opts =＆gt; {opts.TimestampFormat =＆＃34; yyyy-MM-dd HH：mm：ss＆＃34 ;;}））））；使用var registrar = new DependencyInjectionRegistrar（serviceCollection）; var app =新的CommandApp（registrar）; app.Configure（config =＆gt; {config.ValidateExamples（）; config.AddCommand＆lt; ConsoleCommand＆gt;（&&＃34; console＆＃34;）.WithDescription（＆＃34;示例控制台命令。＆＃ 34;）.WithExample（new [] {＆＃34; console＆＃34;}）;}）;返回等待的app.RunAsync（args）;
  ConsoleCommand.cs包含＆＃34; just＆＃34;您的业​​务代码Spectre.Console处理了繁重的解析和验证命令行参数（基于提供的设置类，在后面的更多内容中。），使用依赖注入来解析构造函数参数，等等。域而不是样板代码，从而获得与Azure Function或.NET Workers非常相似的体验，从而可以重用模式和代码。 Spectre.Console支持异步和同步命令。
 使用System.Threading.Tasks；使用Microsoft.Extensions.Logging；使用MyConsoleApp.Commands.Setting；使用Spectre.Console.Cli；命名空间MyConsoleApp.Commands {公共类ConsoleCommand：AsyncCommand＆lt; ConsoleSettings＆gt; {私人ILogger记录器{get; } public over async Task＆lt; int＆gt; ExecuteAsync（CommandContext上下文，ConsoleSettings设置）{Logger.LogInformation（＆＃34;必填：{Mandatory}＆＃34;，settings.Mandatory）； Logger.LogInformation（＆＃34;可选：{Optional}＆＃34 ;, settings.Optional）; Logger.LogInformation（＆＃34; CommandOptionFlag：{CommandOptionFlag}＆＃34 ;, settings.CommandOptionFlag）; Logger.LogInformation（＆＃34; CommandOptionValue：{CommandOptionValue}＆＃34;，settings.CommandOptionValue）；返回等待Task.FromResult（0）; } public ConsoleCommand（ILogger＆lt; ConsoleCommand＆gt; logger）{Logger = logger; }}}
  ConsoleSettings.cs包含每个命令具有哪些参数（如果它们是必需的/可选的），位置以及如何验证的定义。它还包含用于自动生成帮助和错误消息的元数据。
 使用System.ComponentModel；使用Devlead.Console.Commands.Validate；使用Spectre.Console.Cli；命名空间Devlead.Console.Commands.Setting {公共类ConsoleSettings：CommandSettings {[CommandArgument（0，＆＃34;＆lt; mandatory＆gt;＆ ＃34;）] [Description（＆＃34; Mandatory arguments＆＃34;）] public string Mandatory {get;组; } = string.Empty; [CommandArgument（1，＆＃34; [optional]＆＃34;）] [Description（＆＃34; Optional arguments＆＃34;）]公共字符串？可选{get;组; } [CommandOption（＆＃34;-command-option-flag＆＃34;）] [Description（＆＃34; Command option flag。＆＃34;）] public bool CommandOptionFlag {get;组; } [CommandOption（＆＃34;-command-option-value＆lt; value＆gt;＆＃34;）] [Description（＆＃34; Command option value。＆＃34;）] [ValidateString]公共字符串？ CommandOptionValue {get;组; }}}
  Spectre.Console可以通过属性的自定义属性（请参见ConsoleSettings.CommandOptionValue的示例）进行验证，也可以通过覆盖CommandSettings的Validate（）方法进行全局验证。该模板附带一个示例ValidateStringAttribute，该示例仅验证字符串的长度，但是您可以根据需要将其设置为高级。
 使用Spectre.Console；使用Spectre.Console.Cli；命名空间MyConsoleApp.Commands.Validation {public class ValidateStringAttribute：ParameterValidationAttribute {public const int MinimumLength = 3; public ValidateStringAttribute（）：base（errorMessage：null）{}公共重写ValidationResult Validate（ICommandParameterInfo parameterInfo，object？value）＝＆gt; （作为字符串的值）开关{{长度：＆gt; = MinimumLength} =＆gt; ValidationResult.Success（），{长度：＆lt; MinimumLength} =＆gt; ValidationResult.Error（$＆＃34; {parameterInfo？.PropertyName}（{value}）的长度至少应为{MinimumLength}个字符。＆＃34;），_ =＆gt; ValidationResult.Error（$＆＃34;指定了无效的{parameterInfo？.PropertyName}（{value}）。＆＃34;）}; }} 
这是我认为自己做.NET控制台应用程序的快乐之路，请随时告诉我您是否有成功的秘诀，但必须说，我真的很高兴这种组合如何使我编写控制台应用程序 以与我处理.NET工作人员，Azure Functions，ASP .NET Core等相同的方式，确保模式和代码的一致性，较少的重复和良好的重用。 与命令行解析相比，Spectre.Console有很多功能，我强烈建议您检查一下它必须提供的所有其他功能。
note_en:
There's type of application that has followed me since I learned to code in the mid-'80s, and that's the console application. For years they looked the same a  Main(string[] args) and some naive inconsistent command line parser. That gradually improved with the adoption of various OSS helper libraries. In this post, I'll walk through what today is my alternative starting point to  dotnet new console, a way that greatly reduces the boilerplate code needed for logging, parsing, and validation of arguments, letting me focus on the problem to solve and not the plumbing.
  A convenient way to scaffold a new project is using the template function of .NET SDK CLI, it comes preloaded with several templates like  console,  classlib, etc., but beyond that, it's possible to create your own templates, which I've for my and your convenience created, so given  .NET 5 SDK installed, easily yourself can try and take a look at everything discussed in this post.
  So let's get started with creating a new console application according to my opinionated recipe, .NET SDK Templates are distributed as NuGet packages and the canonical source for NuGet packages is  NuGet.org, where I've published my template as  Devlead.Console.Template. Templates are installed using the  dotnet new command with  --install packageId parameter, in this case:
   With the template installed locally, we now have a new  devleadconsole template at our disposal, to create our new console applications with according to me, essential dependencies and boilerplate code:
   MyConsoleApp │ MyConsoleApp.csproj │ Program.cs │ └───Commands │ ConsoleCommand.cs │ ├───Settings │ ConsoleSettings.cs │ └───Validation ValidateStringAttribute.cs
   <Project Sdk="Microsoft.NET.Sdk"> <PropertyGroup> <OutputType>Exe</OutputType> <TargetFramework>net5.0</TargetFramework> <Nullable>enable</Nullable> <TreatWarningsAsErrors>true</TreatWarningsAsErrors> </PropertyGroup> <ItemGroup> <PackageReference Include="Spectre.Console" Version="0.37.0" /> <PackageReference Include="Spectre.Cli.Extensions.DependencyInjection" Version="0.3.0" /> <PackageReference Include="Microsoft.Extensions.Logging" Version="5.0.0" /> <PackageReference Include="Microsoft.Extensions.Logging.Console" Version="5.0.0" /> <PackageReference Include="Microsoft.SourceLink.GitHub" Version="1.0.0" PrivateAssets="All"/> </ItemGroup></Project>
       Nullable with the value  enable, enables the  nullable reference types feature that was introduced with C# 8, making reference types non-nullable by default, basically moving many errors from being caught late at runtime, to be caught early at compile time.
  TreatWarningsAsErrors with the value  true makes the compiler grumpier, it won't just break the build for compiler errors, but also for compiler warnings, combined with  Nullable I personally believe code quality gets better from the start.
  Spectre.Console is a lot of things, a true swiss army for anyone doing console applications, but in this template, it's foremost an extremely opinionated command-line parser.
 Spectre.Cli.Extensions.DependencyInjection makes it easy for  Spectre.Console to interop with standard   Microsoft DependencyInjection, same as used by default for dependency injection with i.e. ASP .NET and Azure Functions.
 Microsoft.Extensions.Logging simplified provides standard logging abstractions and   Microsoft.Extensions.Logging.Console provides an implementation for logging to the console.
 Microsoft.SourceLink.GitHub enables automatic tracking between artifact and source control, providing a better debugging and traceability experience.
  The generated  Program.cs uses the new C#9  Top-level statement pattern removing unnecessary ceremony code from the application, but what it does contain:
  Note:  Top-level statement means as  RunAsync returns a  Task<int>, .NET 5 will automatically generate " Program" class and  async Task<int> Main(string args) for you, removing the need to write a lot of boilerplate code.
 using Microsoft.Extensions.DependencyInjection;using Microsoft.Extensions.Logging;using Devlead.Console.Commands;using Spectre.Console.Cli;using Spectre.Cli.Extensions.DependencyInjection;var serviceCollection = new ServiceCollection() .AddLogging(configure => configure .AddSimpleConsole(opts => { opts.TimestampFormat = "yyyy-MM-dd HH:mm:ss "; }) );using var registrar = new DependencyInjectionRegistrar(serviceCollection);var app = new CommandApp(registrar);app.Configure( config => { config.ValidateExamples(); config.AddCommand<ConsoleCommand>("console") .WithDescription("Example console command.") .WithExample(new[] { "console" }); });return await app.RunAsync(args);
  ConsoleCommand.cs contains "just" your business code,  Spectre.Console handles the heavy lifting of parsing and validating command-line arguments ( based on provided settings class, more on that later in the post.), resolving constructor parameters using dependency injection, etc. Letting you focus on the domain and not the boilerplate code, resulting in a very similar experience to i.e. Azure Function or .NET Workers, enabling reuse of both patterns and code.  Spectre.Console has support for both async and sync commands.
 using System.Threading.Tasks;using Microsoft.Extensions.Logging;using MyConsoleApp.Commands.Setting;using Spectre.Console.Cli;namespace MyConsoleApp.Commands{ public class ConsoleCommand : AsyncCommand<ConsoleSettings> { private ILogger Logger { get; } public override async Task<int> ExecuteAsync(CommandContext context, ConsoleSettings settings) { Logger.LogInformation("Mandatory: {Mandatory}", settings.Mandatory); Logger.LogInformation("Optional: {Optional}", settings.Optional); Logger.LogInformation("CommandOptionFlag: {CommandOptionFlag}", settings.CommandOptionFlag); Logger.LogInformation("CommandOptionValue: {CommandOptionValue}", settings.CommandOptionValue); return await Task.FromResult(0); } public ConsoleCommand(ILogger<ConsoleCommand> logger) { Logger = logger; } }}
  ConsoleSettings.cs contains the definition of what parameters each command has, if they're are mandatory/optional, positional and how they validated. It also contains metadata used for automatically generating help and error messages.
 using System.ComponentModel;using Devlead.Console.Commands.Validate;using Spectre.Console.Cli;namespace Devlead.Console.Commands.Setting{ public class ConsoleSettings : CommandSettings { [CommandArgument(0, "<mandatory>")] [Description("Mandatory argument")] public string Mandatory { get; set; } = string.Empty; [CommandArgument(1, "[optional]")] [Description("Optional argument")] public string? Optional { get; set; } [CommandOption("--command-option-flag")] [Description("Command option flag.")] public bool CommandOptionFlag { get; set; } [CommandOption("--command-option-value <value>")] [Description("Command option value.")] [ValidateString] public string? CommandOptionValue { get; set; } }}
  Spectre.Console can validate either by custom attributes on properties ( see  ConsoleSettings.CommandOptionValue for an example of that) or globally by overriding  Validate() method on  CommandSettings. The template ships with a sample  ValidateStringAttribute that just validates the length of a string, but you can make it as advanced as you want.
 using Spectre.Console;using Spectre.Console.Cli;namespace MyConsoleApp.Commands.Validation{ public class ValidateStringAttribute : ParameterValidationAttribute { public const int MinimumLength = 3; public ValidateStringAttribute() : base(errorMessage: null) { } public override ValidationResult Validate(ICommandParameterInfo parameterInfo, object? value) => (value as string) switch { { Length: >= MinimumLength } => ValidationResult.Success(), { Length: < MinimumLength } => ValidationResult.Error($"{parameterInfo?.PropertyName} ({value}) needs to be at least {MinimumLength} characters long."), _ => ValidationResult.Error($"Invalid {parameterInfo?.PropertyName} ({value}) specified.") }; }}
    This is my opinionated happy path for doing .NET Console applications, feel free to let me know if you've got your own recipe for success, but must say I'm really happy how this combination lets me write console applications in the same way as I do my .NET workers, Azure Functions, ASP .NET Core, etc. ensuring consistency, less duplication and good reuse of both patterns and code. There's a LOT more to  Spectre.Console than command-line parsing, to I hight recommend you check out all the other features it has to offer.

由于员工因抗议工作条件而受到威胁，公司的危险报酬尝试微不足道，而且转瞬即逝
    在冠状病毒大流行期间，美国的亿万富翁的资产净值增加了逾1千亿美元，而他们的许多美国工人在工作场所忍受冠状病毒的风险，在危险条件下工作几乎没有甚至没有额外报酬，还有数百万其他美国人因失业而挣扎求生。
 亚马逊首席执行官兼创始人杰夫·贝佐斯（Jeff Bezos）在2020年冠状病毒大流行期间的净资产增加了超过700亿美元，目前已接近1850亿美元。
  随着亚马逊销量的飙升，亚马逊和亚马逊旗下杂货连锁店Whole Foods的工人抗议不安全的工作条件以及紧跟需求的压力。
  几名参加或领导亚马逊关于工作条件的抗议活动的工人称，他们被报复遭到报复，亚马逊正在与联邦政府作斗争，指控至少有两次违反美国劳工法。
 布鲁金斯学会（Brookings Institution）进行的一项分析显示，到2020年，利润和股东份额飙升了数十亿美元，但亚马逊仅向工人提供了这些额外收入中的一部分危险津贴和奖金。
 亚马逊于2020年6月结束了危险津贴，在大流行期间向工人提供了零星的一次性奖金。在大流行期间，亚马逊工人的平均时薪为0.99美元，而贝佐斯的每小时财富增加了1170万美元。 
“他们认为危险津贴只是为了展示，”马里兰州巴尔的摩的一名亚马逊仓库员工说，他要求保持匿名以免受到报复。 “除非我们愿意每周工作近60个小时，否则我们看不到任何区别。我们几个人别无选择，因为我们是家庭的养家糊口者。我们每天都在恐惧中生活，这是令人气愤的，因为保护我们的努力很少，而高管却坐在家里安全地赚了很多钱。”
 杰西卡·奥托（Jessica Oneto）是全食超市的一名员工，在加利福尼亚州红木城工作了四年，他于10月辞职，部分原因是大流行期间工作条件的疲惫。
 “他们给了我们大约几个月的危险津贴。那只是2美元，随着大流行病的恶化，他们从字面上取走了它。最大的公司之一无力维持下去？”奥托托说。
 特斯拉汽车公司（Tesla Motors）的所有者埃隆·马斯克（Elon Musk）看到他的财产在冠状病毒大流行期间猛增了1400亿美元，并超过贝佐斯（Bezos）成为世界上最富有的人，总净资产为1950亿美元。
 特斯拉的股价在冠状病毒大流行期间飙升，但该公司的员工在今年早些时候遭受了Covid-19爆发，当时该公司在加利福尼亚州弗里蒙特的工厂遭受了骚动，当时马斯克无视当地停工指令重新开放该工厂并重新开始生产。
 马斯克向所有员工发送了一封内部电子邮件，表示如果他们不遵守该命令就去工作，他们将失去失业救济金，并且至少有两名工人因冠状病毒对自己和高危家庭的恐惧而休了无薪假成员因未出席工作而收到终止通知。
 从4月中旬到2020年6月底，特斯拉的受薪雇员的薪资降低了10％至30％。 
弗里蒙特工厂的特斯拉员工说：“虽然小隔间的人呆在家里上班，但我们做不到，也没有得到任何危险津贴。”他要求匿名，以免遭到报复。 “什么也没有变。马斯克有能力做得更多，而他却没有。我发现看到埃隆·马斯克（Elon Musk）的财富在我们承担所有风险的同时增长了多少，真令人心烦。我们收到的只是一封“非常感谢”电子邮件。”
 特斯拉在弗里蒙特市的另一名员工指出，在特斯拉遭到歧视的几起诉讼中，特斯拉对黑人工人的持续虐待。
 这位工人说：“马斯克从未在他的工作场所中解决过这个问题，也没有支持黑人生活问题。” “没有危险津贴或奖金。他们给所有正规工人定期加薪，但由于我在自己的职位上被压倒了，所以我一无所获。”
 亿万富翁比尔·盖茨（Bill Gates）的财富到2020年将增加近180亿美元，达到1310亿美元。尽管盖茨的最初财富来自微软的共同创立，但盖茨的净资产仍通过投资而不断攀升。他的信托基金拥有亚马逊等公司的大量股份，他的投资公司Cascade Investments持有废物管理公司Republic Services超过34％的股份。
 在大流行期间，Republic Services批准了20亿美元的股票回购计划，并向股东支付了3.871亿美元的股利，但该公司的环卫工人并未收到任何危险报酬或奖金，即使由于大量倒闭导致住宅垃圾显着增加。
 “他们在四月或五月初给了我们25美元的礼品卡，仅此而已，”俄亥俄阿克伦区Republic Services的卫生工作人员Yogi Miller说。 “在这段时间里，工人们希望看到他们的薪水有所提高，因为有很多人在此期间被解雇了配偶，现在他们已经成为一个收入家庭。如果我们停止捡垃圾一两个星期，人们就会意识到这对我们的工作至关重要。
note_en:
Companies’ attempts at hazard pay have been paltry and fleeting as employees are threatened for protesting working conditions
    Billionaires in the US have increased their net worth by  more than $1tn during the coronavirus pandemic, while many of their US workers have struggled with coronavirus risks in workplaces, for little to no extra pay to work in hazardous conditions, and millions of other Americans have struggled to survive on unemployment.
 The Amazon CEO and founder, Jeff Bezos,  added more than $70bn to his net worth during the coronavirus pandemic in 2020, which is now  nearly $185bn.
  As Amazon’s sales have soared, workers at  Amazon and Amazon-owned grocery chain Whole Foods have protested against unsafe working conditions and the pressures to keep up with demand.
  Several workers who participated in or led protests at Amazon over working conditions have  alleged they were fired in retaliation and Amazon is  fighting federal complaints alleging at least two of the firings violated US labor laws.
 Profits and stockholder shares have soared through 2020 by billions of dollars, but Amazon has only provided a fraction of those extra earnings in hazard pay and bonuses to workers, according to an  analysis conducted by the Brookings Institution.
 Amazon  ended hazard pay in June 2020, and instead have provided sporadic one-time bonuses to workers during the pandemic. On average, Amazon workers have seen a $0.99-an-hour pay bump during the pandemic, compared with Bezos’s hourly wealth increase of $11.7m.
 “What they considered hazard pay was just for show,” said an Amazon warehouse employee in Baltimore, Maryland who requested to remain anonymous for fear of retaliation. “We couldn’t see a difference unless we were willing to work almost 60 hours a week. Several of us had no choice because we’re the breadwinners of our family. It’s infuriating that we live in fear every day because of minimal efforts to protect us, while executives take in tons of money while sitting safely at home.”
 Jessica Oneto, a Whole Foods employee for four years in Redwood City, California, quit in October due in part to burnout from working conditions during the pandemic.
 “They gave us hazard pay for maybe a couple months. It was only $2 and they literally took it away as the pandemic got worse. One of the biggest companies couldn’t afford to keep it up?” Oneto said.
 Elon Musk, the owner of Tesla Motors, saw his wealth  surge by more than $140bn during the coronavirus pandemic and surpassed Bezos as the  wealthiest person in the world with a total net worth of $195bn.
 Tesla’s share prices have soared during the coronavirus pandemic, but workers for the company have been subjected to a  Covid-19 outbreak at the Fremont, California, plant earlier this year when Musk defied local shutdown orders to reopen the plant and restart production.
 Musk  sent out an internal email to all employees implying they would lose unemployment benefits if they didn’t show up to work in defiance of the order, and at least two workers who took unpaid leave due to coronavirus fears for themselves and at-risk family members  received termination notices for not showing up to work.
 Salaried employees at Tesla had their pay  reduced by 10% to 30% from mid-April to the end of June 2020.
 “While people in cubicles stay home to work, we can’t do that and we don’t get any hazard pay,” said a Tesla employee at the Fremont plant who asked to remain anonymous for fear of retaliation. “Nothing has changed. Musk can afford to do so much more and he doesn’t. I find it sickening to see how much Elon Musk’s wealth has grown while we take all the risks. All we get is a ‘thank you so much’ email.”
 Another Tesla employee in Fremont cited ongoing mistreatment toward Black workers at Tesla amid  several lawsuits alleging discrimination at Tesla, which Tesla has  disputed.
 “Musk has not once addressed this issue in his workplace or  supported Black Lives Matter,” said the worker. “No hazard pay or bonus. They gave all regular workers their regular raise, but being that I’m maxed out at my position I didn’t get anything.”
 Billionaire Bill Gates has seen his wealth  increase by nearly $18bn through 2020, to $131bn. Though his initial fortune stems from co-founding Microsoft, Gates’s net worth has  continued to climb through investments; his trust owns significant holdings in companies such as  Amazon and his investment company Cascade Investments holds  a more than 34% stake in the waste management company Republic Services.
 During the pandemic, Republic Services  approved $2bn in stock buybacks and  paid out $387.1m in dividends to shareholders, but the company’s sanitation workers have not received any hazard pay or bonuses, even as residential trash has  increased significantly due to widespread business closures.
 “They gave us a couple $25 gift cards early in April or May but that was it,” said Yogi Miller, a sanitation worker for Republic Services in the Akron, Ohio, area. “During this time, workers would like to see a little bump in their pay as many people have spouses who have been laid off during this and now they’re a one income family. If we stopped picking up trash for a week or two, people would realize how essential it is the work we do.”
title: 亿万富翁在工人挣扎中大流行期间使净资产增加了1tn
title_en: Billionaires add $1tn to net worth during pandemic as their workers struggle
